sentences.text: I saw plenty.
I would try, because I think I've been thinking it out.
But there are so different incidents that happened to me.
So, anyway, we went down.
We joined this-- we had the petition.
The city is still closed, but they were called-- you know, and they were in-- they kept them in some place.
And now they brought them out to work again.
And we went back with them, and stayed there in this place.
I don't know, whatever it was.
Like maybe a big-- I have no idea.
And we didn't go anywhere, because the city was still-- the town, the ghetto was still closed.
There was no life.
And took another maybe-- another week, and then the city came back to life.
And I discovered that my cousin-- the one who lives here now-- she was alive.
And my mother and another cousin, I think, of mine.
Very, very few.
And there was I.
And, well, the provisions stopped, so what were we doing?
We were waiting for the next one.
But then, they must have moved on to other towns, because, then, the winter was quiet.
The only thing was that, in the meanwhile, my sister was denounced by somebody when she came back one day, and she was shot.
No.
She wasn't even doing anything-- Well, actually, she did.
You know, at that time, my mother was only one left, and we two.
The boys were all gone.
And she was-- and people were becoming desperate.
Well, somebody has to, who has a chance maybe to live to tell the story.
And my mother started insisting that we better.
They were those rumors that you can go to Germany as a Pole, because they are conscripting all the Poles to slave laborers, and, there, your life may be saved.
So we both finally conceded.
And we both went to Warsaw and wanted to volunteer to go to the Polish employment office and volunteer to go to Germany.
My sister insisted-- my mother still had a few things left, and we wanted to leave her with some money.
So my sister insisted, we went.
We had this contact with someone through somebody else.
And we sold the stuff, and she was going back to Miedzyrzec.
And I was waiting for her to come back, and then we would try to go to the German employment office and go.
And I'm waiting, and waiting, and waiting.
You know, I wait for about three, four days, and she doesn't come back.
And this other fellow who was also a Jew, who was our contact who lived in Warsaw also, so he came one day, after two, three days.
He says, look, if Sarah is coming back, I am here.
But I have a proposition for you.
And this after-- an interested-- maybe it's not.
But it is, in a way, how people survived.
So he says, look, I know here this woman, a Jewish woman with a child, three-year-old.
Her husband lives in Sambir with a Polish woman.
She knows that he had a lover, and that he had a child.
And she-- again, you know, she lived in Warsaw as a non-Jew.
This kid was wonderful.
She loved the child.
But somebody is also, you know, what you called them [GERMAN], those Poles who were blackmailing you.
And she decided she cannot hang around in Warsaw anymore, especially with a child.
She is going to go to Sambir.
And I don't know, she was probably was in communication with her husband.
But she's afraid to go by herself.
And this was December.
In case something happens to her, maybe, you know, the child should-- so the proposal was that I should go with her.
She would pay for my trip.
I should go with her to Sambir and see.
Maybe I can live in Sambir.
Maybe, you know, I can work there.
Anyway, so if Sarah comes back-- my sister, if she comes back-- OK, he will be there to tell her where I am.
So we did.
And we, two days later, we takes a train early in the morning.
And we are supposed to change and train in Cracow, and then go on to-- Sambir is all the way east.
And we arrive-- that is that story.
We arrived in Cracow.
And we have to change the train.
We get off the train and we have to wait for the following morning to get the connection to Sambir.
And there we are.
We arrived.
Maybe it was 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock in the evening.
It's dark.
It's cold.
It's winter.
It must have been like the 10th of December, or something like this.
And we don't know.
We want-- we go to the depot.
And there is a depot-- this is true.
There was a depot for Poles, which is not heated.
And there's a depot for the Germans, which is nicely heated.
And at this time, on the train-- when I was saying, I mean, it was a Pole who was saying-- you know, a woman was telling a story about something, and she had some Jewesses stayed with her.
And they were supposed to be such elegant Polish ladies.
And then it turned out one time, one time when she comes home, there is a Gestapo there.
And they turned her whole house upside down because it turned out that these three ladies were Jewesses.
So this man who was sitting next to me, he says, see, to me, it couldn't happen.
I can smell a Jew, you know, 50 miles away.
OK.
So we arrive in Cracow.
And we don't know what to do.
We go into this depot.
It's full of army.
The Poles didn't have an easy time either, with the war.
And it's cold, and the child is crying.
And it's really very, very cold.
And we are supposed to sit there the whole night.
So you know, desperation, we said, well, let's go to whoever-- there is man in charge of-- whatever he is.
And because of the child, maybe he will let us sit in his office, which was warm.
So we go in and we talk to him.
He didn't speak Polish.
And I, me, German Spetz.
I tell him what we really wanted.
And he says, well, I'm sorry I cannot do it.
You know, everybody would ask me to do that.
But then somebody passed by behind the door.
But he says, he is my colleague, who is getting off the duty now.
Maybe he will let you go with him to his apartment and stay overnight there.
And he called this man in.
His name was George Serin.
Ben and I, we went to visit him after the war in Munich, when I was learning it was-- already, it was better.
And because this is such a strange incident.
Again, that's why I think it so happened.
And he tells him, you know, what-- what the situation is.
And he says, of course.
I speak German.
I don't know if she spoke German, too.
She probably did too.
So he says, yes.
If you want to come with me, you can stay overnight in my apartment.
I don't live very far from here.
And we can go there.
And it's nice and warm.
He was one of those employees of that railway, employees.
He was not a soldier.
And he was telling us that he has a wife and three children in Munich.
That's why he understands.
You know how important it is.
And of course, and we are two Polish ladies.
And that's all he does know.
And so he asked us about us.
Where are we going?
And we are telling him we are cousins.
OK.
And this was shortly before Christmas.
Whatever.
So then he takes out a bottle of cognac because we are all frozen.
And he takes out all kinds of food.
And we sit, and we talk.
And of course, not one word of truth came out of our mouth.
But it was really very, very pleasant.
Then he went into his bedroom, and we are remaining there in this couch.
And the little kid, she fell asleep.
And the two of us, we didn't sleep well.
We were just sitting.
And whatever we were talking about, what will happen there.
Anyway, and in the morning, we go back to the depot, take the train, and get to Sambir.
And get to Sambir, and we come into that inn.
That was the woman who owned that inn was a friend of this fellow, of this-- her husband.
And there he is, a tall fellow.
You know, this is Sambir, Ukraine now.
With wearing those high, black boots.
And, you know, he looks really like a real Ukrainian.
And there she comes.
And there am I.
And that inn is full of Germans.
Because this was all the soldiers, all the officers.
And there, you know, we sit down.
And she's serving us a dinner.
And I am Fraulein Anna.
And you have plenty of those, you know, men around you.
Well, I went to-- I rented a little room.
And my friend thought that maybe I could remain there, you know, for the duration of the war.
But then, I didn't like it there at all, especially because I was surrounded by Germans.
And he wasn't my husband.
I didn't even know what happened to them.
And about two or three days later, I decided I'm going back, I'm going back to Miedzyrzec, and I did.
And at strange coincidence, I come to-- I'm coming to Cracow again, and I have to wait again for the train.
And there, again, I'm surrounded by those Poles in the train, you know, who speak only about-- you know, one good thing about the fact that the Jews will be eliminated, what Hitler is doing for them.
And then, when we get up off the train-- must have been about 11 o'clock in the morning.
And this man asked me, do you have-- do you want to rest up for a few hours.
He knows this lady here who is renting out rooms.
And would I go with him?
And I don't see why I shouldn't.
I mean what else would I do?
So we go up there, and he introduces me.
And, you know, and he-- he takes a room, and I take a room.
Oh, this for a couple of hours because the train will leave later.
And I'm resting.
And I think to myself, I don't like the fact that they talk so much about the Jews.
And the fact that-- I felt also restless.
So I decided I'm going to ask.
I want to go and buy myself a toothpaste-- I mean a toothbrush.
I lost it.
And I walked down.
And I leave my little suitcase there.
And I walked down, and I walk maybe two steps, and I met this George Serin.
And he, Fraulein Anna, how is my cousin, and how is a little one?
And he says, where are you going?
I say, I'm going back to Warsaw.
And he says, where are you staying?
And I say, well, you know, I'm staying here for this few hours.
He says, well, let's go back, get your suitcase, and you will stay the rest of the day with me.
And he takes me to lunch, to that-- you know, you won't believe it.
It's really like that movie.
He takes me to the officers mess for lunch.
And there I'm sitting.
And I'm sitting and listening to the officers, and telling each other what they are taking home for their wives.
The best French perfumes, jewelries.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here I am sitting among them.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he, you know, insisted that I go back and get my suitcase.
And he comes with me to the-- you know, this was actually a good alibi.
Because I come-- suddenly, I come back with a German.
And this lady-- so I paid her whatever it was, because-- And that's what happened.
I came back to Miedzyrzec.
I come back to Warsaw.
And my sister didn't appear.
So I just went back to Miedzyrzec.
And there I was till May 1st, when was the deportation.
It's all in the book, what I say.
It's all in Browning's book.
And on the 3rd of May-- and we actually-- we sort of-- we didn't even hide, because, you know, my mother and I we were so-- nobody was left anymore.
You really-- I don't know.
But on the 3rd, I was caught.
I stuck out my head, and I was caught.
And I was put in the train going to [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's where I was pushed out of.
You know, this particular-- everything is so strangely, you know, coincidental.
This particular box car, where I was in, did not have-- didn't have either the barbed wires or the bars.
And I was not the first body that was pushed out.
It was getting so absolutely-- well, we're sitting there all day long at the market, and they were shooting in.
And then they finally, you know, pushed us into this train.
I don't even know what happened to my mother.
I didn't even know where she was.
And it was so, really, impossible.
So somebody started picking up bodies and just pushing them out to get some more room.
The Germans were standing on those steps and firing in, just for the fun of it.
You have no idea.
If one didn't see such-- you cannot imagine what it looked like.
So I flew out.
It was the middle of the night.
The train was moving.
Of course, it was moving.
And in a funny way, as I say, everything sounds so-- almost like a fairy tale.
I fell sideways into a ditch.
Because, you know, along the railway.
And I don't know how long I was lying.
So when I opened my eyes, it was already getting a little bit light.
And I looked around.
And I started touching myself.
And I didn't even have a scratch.
So I just-- for a while, I was sitting.
And I was thinking, what should I do.
And I say, the only thing for me to do is to try to walk back to the ghetto, to Miedzyrzec and wait for the next deportation, or whatever else happens.
And I actually was almost sure that somebody will, on the way, somebody will-- a Gestapo man, or a Pole will denounce me.
Or like you said before, somebody will want to rape me.
Nobody wanted do anything.
It sounds funny.
Anyway, it took me about 15 days.
I-- when I started walking, there were two peasants going to their fields.
And I asked them where I am.
And they told me I am not far from Lublin, and how far to Miedzyrzec.
And they told me about, I don't know, 100 kilometers, 80 kilometers.
Anyway, it took me 15 days.
And I slept in chicken coops.
I slept in barns.
Sometimes, you got soaked with the rain, you know.
It was May.
Sometimes, I would stop off in a hut, you know, knock on the door, and somebody-- some peasant woman would give me a piece of bread.
Sometimes, they were chasing me with dogs.
I don't admitting anything.
But, you know, but the Poles had a hard time too, by then.
Those that were being sent to Germany as slave laborers.
They were-- things were taken away from them.
You know, they were being rounded up.
The trains were being rounded up.
See, I was not so only one on the road.
You know, it was-- really, it was-- it was really a war condition.
And one has to see it in order-- you know, when people were smuggling.
People were-- sometimes, especially from the trains, you know, people were rounded up.
And taking away whatever they were smuggling.
Those big ports, or whatever.
They were sent off to Germany.
Some of them were sent to-- well, they were working camps.
[GERMAN].

Some of them, like I, were sent-- I actually probably, when it comes to conditions under the war, I probably had the best one that one can-- if there was such a thing as choice, under the circumstances.
And whether one wanted to choose it.
I was a cleaning girl in a civilian hospital in Stuttgart, a women's civilian hospital run by nuns, by Protestant nuns.
One year in Stuttgart, and then Stuttgart was bombed out.
Let me tell you.
I did get back to the ghetto.
And it's always with [NON-ENGLISH].
And I bumped into [NON-ENGLISH].
She was still there.
The same one with whom we were lying in this thing.
She survived.
And when I finally got into the ghetto, you know, it was, of course, it was stolen to the ghetto.
Then a man-- there were very few people left.
Maybe 150 Jews were still left in the ghetto by then.
It was the end of May.
And I met-- I don't remember his name now-- who knew my family.
And he says, well-- I told him, you know, where I come from.
And he says, well, you come to our room.
You'll be with us.
And there we were.
And what are you going to do?
And nothing.
We are waiting for the next deportation.
And everybody was really very much ready for it.
And then, one day, I met [NON-ENGLISH] on the street again.
And she comes to me and tells me a story, that she heard that one of the-- there were those Polish employment office, employee part of the German employment office.
That if you, for a bribe, one of them, whatever his name was.
I don't know.
He may send you to Germany as a slave laborer on false papers.
And you may survive this way.
And I told her, look, I don't have any money to pay anybody anything.
I don't even know what I-- what I really-- I wish sometimes I would remember what I wore, how filthy I was, eaten up by lice.
But she says, if I find the Jewish policeman who will go-- because he cannot get out of the ghetto.
I mean everything is punishable by death, especially at that time.
Would you go with me?
And I said fine.
And maybe two, three days later, she got hold of a Jewish fellow she knew as a policeman.
And he walks out with us.
And he goes with us to this fellow's address.
And this fellow's not even home.
And we walk back.

And, again, you know, that's what I consider so strange, those little things that sound as if somebody would-- you couldn't even-- I don't think I could dream it up, because I didn't have such a fantasy.
And we're walking-- you know, we're walking back to the ghetto, and we pass another street on the way to the ghetto.
And this policeman, we pass her by.
And the policeman says, here lives another Polish employee of the employment office.
Once you are out, maybe you want to ask him.
And [NON-ENGLISH] rings a bell.
And the man opens the door and looks at us.
And very politely says, what can I do for you.
And [NON-ENGLISH] tells him this story.
And he looks at us and he says, well, I would never do it.
It would jeopardize my life.
And this, and that, and the other thing.
And then, I know his name.
His name was George, whatever [NON-ENGLISH] something.
And he says-- he looks at me and he says, but I have a suggestion to you.
You don't look Jewish.
We are desperate to send people to Germany.
We are having a hard time getting them.
They are hiding.
They are giving us all false information, this and that.
If you want to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] and volunteer, and if nobody will have any suspicion of who you are, they will send you off without anything, and you may save your life.
And that's really how it was.
And that's another thing.
And [NON-ENGLISH], she didn't want to do it.
Actually, she was a very beautiful young girl.
But she had dark hair and brown eyes, or whatever.
But she, again, asks him to-- there was a Polish family in [NON-ENGLISH], which was not far from [NON-ENGLISH].
And she thought if she could get-- this was good friends, before the war, of her family, that if she could get to this family, they probably would save her.
Because it was really, you know, it was getting sort of yes and no late in the war.
The Poles were suffering a lot also.
So what she would need is a permit to take the train from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH].
And he says, yes, this he can do for her.
So I come back to the ghetto, and I tell everybody.
And they're like, you have to, you have to.
Somebody, if not-- And I still believe, maybe, if there were no survivors, the Holocaust might have never been told, in spite of all those historians.
And that's what I want to tell you.
If I witness something, and the next day, somebody from the-- who also walking in the-- you know, those tremendous warehouses, those Jewish warehouses?
They were cleaning out the houses, and there were Jewish inmates who were working.
They were separating thing this and that and the other.
There was a tremendous work.
And one of them who works there asked me to come with her.
We're going to select, to choose a few, a blouse, a skirt, a coat, shoes.
I mean that was-- and we go.
And there were German women Gestapos standing.
And a young boy-- I don't know if he was also one of the workers there.
And somebody must have dropped something to eat, and he, right in front of my eyes, he bent down to pick it up.
And this woman Nazi put, immediately, a bullet through his head.
And there he was, lying in front of me.
And here I go in to save my life.
I was almost ready to go back.
But, again, we talked about it.
So here I am.
And that's really what happened.
Everybody, you have to, you have to, and washed me, and scrubbed me.
Iron the clothes, what, I mean, selected it to fit me.
And the next day, I went to the employment office, and I come in, and some Pole gets up.
And she says, what do I want.
And I say, I want to volunteer to go to Germany.
So the German head of the office, when he heard her tell somebody who wants to apply, free willing.
He called me to his desk.
And first he asked me, why do you want to go.
And I said, I am an orphan, and I was smuggling till now.
And the last times didn't work anymore.
So I decided I really have to work for it.
And he gives me, you know, glorious picture of how a will live in Germany.
I will go to a beautiful city called Stuttgart.
And I will-- he said I would be working in a restaurant.
And I would have enough food to eat, and so forth, and so on.
And that really-- and call somebody and ask him to fill out my papers.
And I had my name, Anna Wojcik, I had all those things prepared.
I couldn't find it.
I hid it somewhere.
And they didn't even ask me and give me all the document.

And then the train ticket-- I have to be at 6 o'clock in the morning, the following day, at the station to go to [NON-ENGLISH,] no, to go to Lublin, which was sort of a transit place where I'm going to go through medical examination.
And since I have already a place where to go, to Stuttgart, I won't even have to wait, because they were usually sending out contingents of 100.
I won't even have to wait, which was important to me, to be among 100 Poles, waiting.
And I will be there, you know, in two, three days.
And that's really what happened.
And when I came to Stuttgart, and I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], so they gave me an assignment.
There came a nun.
And she says, well, you will be going to the-- you will be working in the civilian hospital.
You will be [GERMAN], domestic help.
And that's what it was.
And, I mean, as far as-- the only-- I remember, when I arrived there, they took me in.
They gave me some lunch.
And they wanted to know about who I am, and what I am, and so forth and so on.
And she said, would you like to take a bath.
Would I?
And I was worried.
You know, for some time, I was worried about the lice.
I was so full of lice.
Under my arms, the whole body, everything.
And the conditions there, as far as working conditions-- so there I worked with three other Polish girls, and four Ukrainian girls.
And we lived in the attic.
And all of them worked in the kitchen.
And I was only one-- I don't-- you know, it's really-- it's a matter of luck, I suppose.
And also, because the others were peasant girls, and I was sort of, you know, a city slicker.
I worked with another German girl, who was actually training to be a nurse, but they had to go through all those stages.
So she really-- she really taught me how to clean.
And then she left, and another one came.
And then, we worked in the hospital-- many times it was bombed out.
And then, but-- we-- I suppose most of the Poles felt that.
But you really were not very much afraid of this bombing.
You were hoping that it will bring the end of the war, though we were always the last ones.
That time, it was about 10 minutes past 2 o'clock in the morning.
And there was always this warden that was going through the floors to tell everybody to go to the-- to the [GERMAN], to the shelter.
And they were so well-organized, the Germans.
The patients, their beds, everything underground was the same, everything like above ground.
The same floors, the same beds, the same everything for the patients.
Oh, again?
That was so fast.
I am finishing now.
